title: Local diagnostics for strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking and non-equilibrium phase transitions
abstract: We construct strongly $\mathbb{Z}_2$-symmetric local Markov/Lindblad dynamics exhibiting transitions between strong-paramagnetic behavior and strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking. In 1+1d, an absorbing-state construction gives a transition with scaling consistent with the parity-conserving branching-annihilating random-walk universality class. In 2+1d, a pair-flip variant of Toom's rule provides evidence for a stable strong-paramagnetic/weakly symmetry-broken regime and a transition into an active SWSSB regime. We also introduce a marginal fidelity correlator of radius $R$, a local proxy for the usual SWSSB fidelity order parameter requiring tomography only on $\mathcal{O}(R^d)$-size regions. For broad classes of states, including symmetry-projected Gibbs-like states satisfying suitable local indistinguishability assumptions, we bound the error between the marginal and global fidelity correlators by terms decaying exponentially in $R$. These marginal fidelities provide a model-independent diagnostic of SWSSB in absorbing-state transitions, where microscopic notions of defects or activity are not universal.
summary: This paper introduces the marginal fidelity correlator as a robust, local diagnostic tool to study spontaneous symmetry breaking in non-equilibrium quantum systems. By applying this tool to 1+1d and 2+1d lattice models, the authors confirm its ability to detect transitions between paramagnetic and symmetry-broken phases, offering a general method applicable where traditional defect counting fails.
main_problem: The paper investigates local diagnostics for phase transitions, specifically the transition from a strong-paramagnetic (SP) phase to a strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking (SWSSB) phase in non-equilibrium quantum systems.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrate that a local fidelity correlator serves as a model-independent diagnostic for SWSSB, showing consistency with known universality classes in 1+1d and providing evidence for stable regimes in 2+1d.
method: The primary method involves constructing and analyzing local Markov/Lindblad dynamics, utilizing a novel 'marginal fidelity correlator' as a local proxy for the global fidelity order parameter.
model_or_system: The study uses strongly Z2-symmetric local Markov/Lindblad dynamics in 1+1d and 2+1d lattices. The dynamics are tuned between different phases (SP, SWSSB) using interpolating Lindbladians or pair-flip Toom's rules.
key_observables: Marginal fidelity correlator ($F_R^{ij}$), error density ($n_-$), sample-averaged absolute magnetization ($\langle|m|angle$), and Binder cumulant.
important_parameters: The radius $R$ of the local region, the system dimension (1+1d, 2+1d), and the tuning parameter $\alpha$ controlling the dynamics.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on local indistinguishability assumptions to bound the error between marginal and global fidelity correlators, and the effective classical nature of the dynamics in some contexts.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate finite size scaling collapses for the order parameter and marginal fidelity correlator matching PC-BARW exponents in 1+1d, and show phase diagrams and magnetization convergence for the 2+1d model.
paper_structure: The paper develops the diagnostic tool (marginal fidelity correlator), applies it to 1+1d and 2+1d models exhibiting SP-to-SWSSB transitions, and compares its results against established order parameters and theoretical expectations for symmetry breaking.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, local, and model-independent diagnostic tool (the fidelity correlator) for detecting subtle non-equilibrium phase transitions like SWSSB, which is highly relevant for understanding open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The 1+1d dynamics is designed to interpolate between L+ and LSWSSB. L+ has exact steady states ρ0 and ρW in the even and odd strong Z2 sectors respectively. LSWSSB has an exact steady state space spanned by three steady states. All even sector states orthogonal to ρ0 get taken to ρ+ at late times, so ρ0 is exponen- tially unlikely. (b) The finite size scaling collapse gives critical ex- ponents β/ν⊥= 0.53, 1/ν⊥= 0.56 for the order parameter n−(in- set) that match well with the PC-BARW universality class (β/ν⊥= 0.5, 1/ν⊥= 0.54 [22]) with αc = 0.44. The radius R = 3 2-point marginal fidelity correlator also demonstrates finite size scaling col- lapse with similar exponents: β/ν⊥=...

FIG. 2. (a) The marginal one-point fidelity correlator takes the re- duced density matrix on R(i) ∪R(j), which includes 2(2R + 1) sites. In higher dimensions, we specify Rx and Ry, and take the re- duced density matrix on a region of size (2Rx +1)(2Ry +1) around i and j. (b) The fidelity correlator as a function of R and Rx shows rapid convergence; see Ref. [11] for calculations supporting faster convergence in the SWSSB phase and in the 2+1d case for Rx > 0 away from βc. The first plot tracks radius R fidelity correlator for a system of size L = 112 across the 1+1d phase transition. The sec- ond plot tracks Rx fidelity correlator for a system of size L2 = 400 across the 2+1d strict pair...

FIG. 3. (a) The purple circles represent + spins and white circles represent −spins, and the blue links represent pair flips allowed for strict pair-flip Toom. (b) Two possible phase diagrams for the 2+1d model with strict pair-flip Toom. (c) In the odd sector in the SP phase, we should have ⟨|m|⟩= m∞−c/L2 with m∞= 1 and c/2 giving the size of a wandering O(1) minority domain, so we fit m∞and c using L = 10, 12, 16, 20 data (in the plot we use m instead of m∞to save space). The strict pair flip Toom rule (blue) demonstrates a convincing stable phase where m∞= 1 whereas the chill pair flip Toom rule has m∞very close to but not quite 1 for very small α. We also obtain data points (not shown...

FIG. 1. The purple circles represent + spins and the white circles represent −spins. We focus on the Toom unstable spin circled in pink, and show the available strict pair-flip Toom moves in blue and chill pair-flip Toom rules in pink.

FIG. 2. We take the same coloring conventions as in Fig. 1 and focus on the Toom unstable spin circled in pink, assuming that beyond the illustrated ones are all +. (a) and (b) give examples of configurations where strict pair-flip Toom pushes the two minority spins together into a larger domain or completely annihilates the two minority spins, while chill pair flip Toom allows the minority spins to get farther apart with probability 5/8 (a) or 7/8 (b). In (c), chill pair-flip Toom can split the minority domain into two small islands, that can then wander away from each other.
